name: When to Stop Being Polite — and What That Actually Looks Like
description: 99% of calls succeed with patience, warmth, and curiosity. A small minority require a hard pivot: firm refusal, named consequences, regulatory escalation language, or immediate disengagement. This skill defines the narrow triggers for that pivot, and what 'no longer polite' looks like in practice — which is NOT rude, hostile, or threatening, but is deliberately formal, citing-rights, and unambiguous about non-cooperation.

context.when_to_use: ONLY when a clearly bright-line condition is met (see triggers). Default posture remains warm and collaborative — this skill is the exception, not the rule.
preconditions: Agent has confirmed at least one trigger condition with a specific recent example, not a vague sense.
Agent has authority from the user to formally escalate, lodge complaints, or disengage.
Agent has the user's relevant rights / protections clear (jurisdiction, regulator, statute if known).

principles: Politeness is leverage. You spend politeness when you've truly run out of other options — not when you're frustrated.
'Stopping being polite' does not mean becoming rude. It means becoming FORMAL — citing rights, naming consequences, declining to play within the framing the rep is using.
Hostility is never useful. Anger is never useful. Naming a specific regulator, requesting a specific form, or invoking a specific right IS useful.
Triggers must be SPECIFIC: a concrete observable behaviour, not a feeling. 'They were rude' is not a trigger; 'They lied about a written policy I have in hand' is.
Once you escalate formally, you cannot un-escalate cheaply. Make sure you mean it.
Even in the formal mode, leave the rep an off-ramp. People who feel cornered escalate; people who see a graceful exit comply.

phrases.name_the_trigger: I want to flag something. You just said [X], and the written [bill/contract/email] in front of me says [Y]. That gap is significant.
phrases.request_formal_record: I'd like this conversation noted formally in the account record. Could you confirm a note has been added with [specific summary]?
phrases.decline_to_proceed: Given that, I don't want to continue on this call. I'd like a supervisor, or I'll end the call and take this through [written / regulatory] channels.
phrases.regulatory_invocation: If we can't resolve this here, my next step is to file a complaint with [CFPB / state insurance commissioner / FCC / state AG / FTC]. I'd rather not — could we try one more path first?
phrases.rights_invocation_billing: Under [FCBA / FCRA / state statute], I'm formally disputing this charge. Please log the dispute, hold collection activity, and send confirmation in writing.
phrases.rights_invocation_debt: If this is a debt-collection call, I'm asking under the FDCPA that you cease contacting me by phone and communicate only in writing.
phrases.hard_disengage: I'm going to end this call now. Thank you.

tactics.name: Confirm a hard trigger, not a soft frustration
tactics.when: Before pivoting out of the warm mode.
tactics.script: Triggers: (a) caught lie about a written fact you have in hand; (b) rep refusing identity verification on inbound call that's asking for sensitive info; (c) threats from the rep ('we'll send to collections today unless...'); (d) refusal to escalate AND refusal to provide refusal-source; (e) extracting personal data not relevant to the stated purpose; (f) repeated refusal across two calls + one supervisor on a clearly bright-line right. Anything else: stay warm.

tactics.name: Shift to formal register without becoming hostile
tactics.script: Slow down. Speak more precisely. Use full sentences. Name the specific behaviour. Stop using filler words like 'sorry' and 'just'. Tone is still calm — the change is in the formality, not the volume.

tactics.name: Name the gap on the record
tactics.script: Use `name_the_trigger`. The point is to put the rep on notice that the conversation is now being treated formally, while still giving them a chance to correct.

tactics.name: Invoke the specific right or regulator
tactics.script: Use `rights_invocation_billing` / `rights_invocation_debt` / `regulatory_invocation`. Cite the SPECIFIC statute or agency that applies. Generic threats ('I'll report you') carry less weight than 'I will file an FCC complaint reference Title 47, Section X'.

tactics.name: Demand a formal account note
tactics.script: Use `request_formal_record`. Make sure the company's own record reflects the dispute. This is critical for any later legal or regulatory action.

tactics.name: Leave an off-ramp
tactics.script: Offer ONE more path: a supervisor, a written summary, a 24-hour pause. Reps who see a graceful resolution take it. Reps who are cornered double down.

tactics.name: Hard disengage if needed
tactics.when: Rep escalates abuse, persists in clear violation, or you are no longer being heard.
tactics.script: Use `hard_disengage`. Do not slam. Do not insult. End. Document. Take it to writing or regulator.

alternative_interpretations.observation: Rep is curt / unfriendly / having a bad day.
consider: Bad day, no policy issue — stay polite, do not escalate. This is NOT a trigger.
Hostile but providing correct service — stay polite.
Hostile AND withholding service the user is entitled to — combination triggers, but the hostility is not the reason; the withholding is.
Mirror-induced — you are being curt and they are matching you.
alternative_interpretations.next_action: Reset your own warmth first. Most 'rude reps' soften when met with consistent calm.

alternative_interpretations.observation: Rep states something you can verify in writing is false.
consider: Rep is mistaken, not malicious — usually true. Surface gently first per `detect-lies-and-contradictions`.
Rep is improvising under pressure — same.
Rep is following a script known to misrepresent — material; escalate formally if it costs the user money.
Rep is deliberately deceiving — rare; treat as material if pattern is clear.
alternative_interpretations.next_action: Try `detect-lies-and-contradictions` first. Only formally escalate if (a) the misstatement is material AND (b) the rep refuses to correct after one clean opportunity.

alternative_interpretations.observation: Rep threatens consequences ('we'll send this to collections', 'we'll terminate your service today').
consider: Standard, lawful collections language — not a trigger by itself.
Premature threat — would violate billing dispute rules if a dispute is on file.
Genuine final notice — verify against any written notices the user has.
Designed to extract immediate payment to circumvent normal dispute process.
alternative_interpretations.next_action: Calmly ask: 'When was the written notice sent and what does it say?' If under active billing dispute, invoke `rights_invocation_billing`.

alternative_interpretations.observation: Your own emotional temperature is rising.
consider: Trigger is real and your reaction is calibrated — proceed but keep volume down.
Trigger is real and your reaction is overcalibrated — pause; cool down before escalating.
Trigger is not real and you're frustrated — DO NOT escalate; you'll regret it.
Manipulation working in reverse — rep deliberately provoking you so they can label you 'difficult'.
alternative_interpretations.next_action: Take a 10-second silent beat. Confirm the trigger is observable, specific, and recent. If yes, proceed formally. If no, stay polite or end the call cleanly.

boundaries: NEVER raise your voice, threaten the rep personally, or use profanity. Formality has more force than anger.
NEVER threaten to 'report' someone vaguely. Either cite a specific regulator or don't invoke escalation.
NEVER invoke statute language you don't understand — get it right or don't say it.
DO disengage rather than fight, when the call is no longer recoverable.
DO loop the user in immediately if the formal mode is entered — they should know their call has crossed a threshold.
success_signals: Rep self-corrects after the gap is named.
Supervisor takes over and recovers the situation.
Formal note is added to the account.
Rep agrees to send a written summary acknowledging the dispute.
Threatened action (collections, termination) is paused while dispute is open.
failure_signals: Rep doubles down on the contested behaviour.
Supervisor confirms the rep's position without acknowledging the cited right.
Rep refuses to add a formal note.
Call ends with no record of the dispute.

exit_strategy.on_success: Capture the corrected outcome in writing. Confirm the formal note. Step back to a polite register if the rest of the call continues — formality is a tool, not a default.
exit_strategy.on_failure: End the call cleanly. Move to written channels: certified letter, regulatory complaint, executive customer service. Provide the user with a clear summary including (a) what was alleged, (b) what was said by the rep, (c) what right or rule applies, (d) recommended next channel.
follow_ups: If regulator was invoked, follow through — empty threats train future reps to ignore the invocation.
Pull all evidence collected per `mid-call-evidence-collection` for any complaint filing.

required_user_info: Jurisdiction and relevant regulators / statutes the user is willing to invoke.
Explicit authority to escalate formally on the user's behalf.
Any prior dispute filings, written notices, or contractual provisions that bear on the trigger.
